91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	Archon talks back to the user through **`TO_USER.md`** — a single, *persistent* notice board surfaced as a UI banner. It is **shared and writable by every agent** (plan, prover, review). It is no longer cleared at the start of each review phase; content survives across iters until an agent prunes it. The discipline every writer follows:
112	
113	- **Concise**: the whole file stays ≤ 2–3 short bullets. It is a banner, not a log.
114	- **Relevant now**: before adding anything, read the current content and **delete any bullet that is no longer true at this point** (the blocker was resolved, the decision was overtaken, the credential got set). Never let it accumulate stale notices.
115	- **Notice board, never a question queue**: the loop is autonomous and may run unattended for many iters. Never write "awaiting your decision", an options menu, a "where to reply", or "default to X if no reply" — anything implying the loop is paused for a human. State decisions as *made* and blockers as *worked-around-as-far-as-possible*; the user steers asynchronously via `USER_HINTS.md`.

1	\chapter{The Quot scheme}
2	\label{chap:Picard_QuotScheme}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/QuotScheme.lean
4	
5	% STRATEGY NOTE. This chapter packages the Quot-foundations layer of the
6	% Grothendieck--Altman--Kleiman Quot-scheme construction
7	% \(\mathrm{Quot}^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\): the per-fiber Hilbert polynomial, the
8	% Quot functor of \(T\)-flat coherent quotients of \(E_T\) on
9	% \(X_T = X \times_S T\), and the Grassmannian \emph{scheme} together with
10	% its representability. The Quot functor underlies the relative Picard
11	% functor via the Hilbert scheme \(\Hilb_{C/k} = \Quot_{\mathcal{O}_C/C/k}\)
12	% -- the case \(E = \mathcal{O}_X\) of the Quot functor.
13	%
14	% LOAD-BEARING SUB-PREREQUISITE. The Quot construction embeds Quot as a
15	% locally closed subfunctor of a Grassmannian
16	% \(\mathrm{Grass}(W \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_S} \mathrm{Sym}^r V, \Phi(r))\).
17	% Mathlib (at the pinned commit) carries no Grassmannian \emph{scheme} -- only
18	% a linear-algebra Grassmannian as a finite-rank-quotient variety. The
19	% scheme-level Grassmannian over an arbitrary noetherian base is therefore an
20	% in-project sub-build. This chapter introduces it as
21	% \cref{def:grassmannian_scheme} and states its representability
22	% (\cref{thm:grassmannian_representable}).
23	
24	\section{Setup and motivation}
25	\label{sec:quot_setup}
26	
27	Throughout this chapter, let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme, \(\pi : X \to S\) a
28	projective morphism, \(L\) a relatively very ample line bundle on \(X\) over \(S\),
29	and \(E\) a coherent sheaf on \(X\). The Quot construction packages the
30	\(T\)-flat coherent quotients \(E_T \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\) of the
31	pull-back \(E_T\) to \(X_T = X \times_S T\), modulo the equivalence
32	\(\langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle = \langle \mathcal{F}', q'\rangle\) defined by
33	\(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\). Fixing the Hilbert polynomial
34	\(\Phi(\lambda) = \chi(\mathcal{F}|_{X_t}(\lambda)) \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\)
35	(constant on connected \(T\) by flatness)
36	stratifies the Quot functor into pieces \(\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\), each of
37	which Grothendieck proved is representable by a projective \(S\)-scheme.
38	This chapter develops the foundations of that picture: the Hilbert
39	polynomial (\cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}), the Quot functor
40	(\cref{def:quot_functor}), and the Grassmannian scheme
41	(\cref{def:grassmannian_scheme}) with its representability
42	(\cref{thm:grassmannian_representable}).
43	
44	% SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1;
45	% FGA Explained Ch.~5, AMS MSM 123 (read from
46	% references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L287-L500).
47	% The Hartshorne reference for the polynomial-eventually
48	% property of Euler characteristics is [Hartshorne], III.5.2 (the scanned
49	% PDF at references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf has no text layer, so
50	% the verbatim source-quote is taken from
51	% Nitsure \S 1; Hartshorne III.5.2 is cited in prose as the textbook
52	% reference).

54	\section{Hilbert polynomial of a coherent sheaf}
55	\label{sec:hilbert_polynomial}
56	
57	\begin{definition}\leanok
58	[Hilbert polynomial]
59	  \label{def:hilbert_polynomial}
60	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.hilbertPolynomial}
61	  \uses{thm:hilbertPoly_of_sectionModule}
62	  % ENCODING. `def:hilbert_polynomial` is formalized via the GRADED HILBERT
63	  % FUNCTION of the section module ⊕_{m≥0} Γ(X_s, F_s ⊗ L_s^{⊗m})
64	  % (def:sectionGradedModule), whose polynomiality is extracted in
65	  % thm:hilbertPoly_of_sectionModule from the graded Hilbert–Serre rationality
66	  % lemma (lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational) together with the Mathlib lemma
67	  % Polynomial.existsUnique_hilbertPoly (lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib). The
68	  % construction uses only H^0; for m≫0 it agrees with the Euler characteristic
69	  % χ by Serre vanishing, so it is the classical Hilbert polynomial and no
70	  % numerical invariant is lost.

102	  be a line bundle on \(X\). For a coherent sheaf \(\mathcal{F}\) on \(X\) whose
103	  schematic support is proper over \(S\) and a point \(s \in S\), write \(X_s\)
104	  for the fibre over \(s\), \(\mathcal{F}_s = \mathcal{F}|_{X_s}\) and
105	  \(L_s = L|_{X_s}\). The \emph{Hilbert polynomial of \(\mathcal{F}\) at \(s\)
106	  relative to \(L\)} is the unique polynomial
107	  \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s} \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\) that agrees, for all
108	  \(m \gg 0\), with the graded Hilbert function
109	  \[
110	    m \;\longmapsto\; \dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma\bigl(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}\bigr)
111	  \]

116	  \(\bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\)
117	  (\cref{def:sectionGradedRing}). Existence and uniqueness of
118	  \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\) are the content of
119	  \cref{thm:hilbertPoly_of_sectionModule}.

121	  This is the classical Hilbert polynomial. When \(\mathcal{F}_s\) is coherent
122	  and \(L_s\) is ample on the proper \(\kappa(s)\)-scheme \(X_s\), Serre vanishing
123	  gives \(H^i(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}) = 0\) for all \(i > 0\)
124	  and \(m \gg 0\), so for \(m \gg 0\)
125	  \[
126	    \dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})
127	    \;=\; \chi(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})
128	    \;=\; \sum_{i \ge 0} (-1)^i \dim_{\kappa(s)} H^i(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}),
129	  \]

131	  Hilbert polynomial of Snapper's Lemma -- no numerical invariant is lost. The
132	  construction above, however, uses only the global sections \(H^0\): the higher
133	  cohomology enters solely through the classical statement that the two functions
134	  agree for \(m \gg 0\), never through the definition of \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\)
135	  itself. When \(\mathcal{F}\) is \(S\)-flat the function
136	  \(s \mapsto \Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\) is locally constant on \(S\), so \(\Phi\) is
137	  well-defined on connected components.
138	\end{definition}

140	\section{Graded Hilbert polynomial}
141	\label{sec:graded_hilbert_polynomial}
142	
143	This section gives the graded-Hilbert-function construction that underlies
144	\cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}. Working over a single fibre \(X_s\) with its
145	ample line bundle \(L_s\) and coherent sheaf \(\mathcal{F}_s\), it builds the
146	section graded ring (\cref{def:sectionGradedRing}) and module
147	(\cref{def:sectionGradedModule}), records their finite generation
148	(\cref{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg}), and extracts the Hilbert polynomial
149	(\cref{thm:hilbertPoly_of_sectionModule}) by combining the graded
150	Hilbert--Serre rationality of the Hilbert series
151	(\cref{lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational}) with Mathlib's polynomial-extraction
152	lemma (\cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}). The route uses only \(H^0\)
153	(global sections), so it is independent of higher coherent cohomology.
154	
155	\begin{definition}
156	[Section graded ring]
157	  \label{def:sectionGradedRing}
158	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.sectionGradedRing}
159	  % NOTE: Formalization is blocked on the absence of tensor products for sheaves of modules in
160	  % Mathlib at the pinned commit. The degree-\(m\) component \(\Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\)
161	  % requires a tensor power \(L_s^{\otimes m}\) of a line bundle; this infrastructure (tensor
162	  % product of sheaves of modules with lax-monoidal global sections) is not yet available, making
163	  % this definition a prerequisite sub-build.
164	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
165	  % sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert Polynomials" (read from
166	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L453-L464).
167	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
168	  % "Let $X$ be a finite type scheme over
169	  %  a field $k$, together with a line bundle $L$.
170	  %  Recall that if $F$ is a coherent sheaf on
171	  %  $X$ whose support is proper over $k$, then
172	  %  the \textbf{Hilbert polynomial} ${\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]$ of $F$ is defined by
173	  %  the function
174	  %  $${\Phi}(m) = \chi(F(m)) =
175	  %  \sum_{i=0}^n (-1)^i \dim_k H^i(X, F\otimes L^{\otimes m})$$
176	  %  where the dimensions of the cohomologies are finite because of the
177	  %  coherence and properness conditions."
178	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5); the section graded
179	  ring is the homogeneous coordinate ring of [Hartshorne], I.7.}
180	  Let \(s \in S\) and let \(L_s\) be a line bundle on the fibre \(X_s\), a scheme
181	  of finite type over the residue field \(\kappa(s)\). The \emph{section graded
182	  ring} of \(L_s\) is the graded commutative ring
183	  \[
184	    R(X_s, L_s) \;=\; \bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma\bigl(X_s,\, L_s^{\otimes m}\bigr),
185	  \]
186	  with degree-\(m\) component \(\Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\) and multiplication
187	  the tensor product of sections
188	  \(\Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m}) \otimes_{\kappa(s)}
189	  \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m'}) \to \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes (m+m')})\),
190	  \(\sigma \otimes \tau \mapsto \sigma \cdot \tau\). Its degree-zero component is
191	  \(\Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{O}_{X_s})\), which contains \(\kappa(s)\). When \(X_s\) is
192	  proper over \(\kappa(s)\) and \(L_s\) is ample, \(R(X_s, L_s)\) is a finitely
193	  generated -- hence Noetherian -- graded \(\kappa(s)\)-algebra (a suitable
194	  Veronese power of \(L_s\) is very ample and embeds \(X_s\) into a projective
195	  space, exhibiting \(R(X_s, L_s)\) up to a finite extension as a quotient of a
196	  polynomial ring).
197	\end{definition}

199	\begin{definition}
200	[Section graded module]
201	  \label{def:sectionGradedModule}
202	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.sectionGradedModule}
203	  \uses{def:sectionGradedRing}
204	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
205	  % Polynomials" (read from
206	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L453-L464).
207	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
208	  % "Recall that if $F$ is a coherent sheaf on
209	  %  $X$ whose support is proper over $k$, then
210	  %  the \textbf{Hilbert polynomial} ${\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]$ of $F$ is defined by
211	  %  the function
212	  %  $${\Phi}(m) = \chi(F(m)) =
213	  %  \sum_{i=0}^n (-1)^i \dim_k H^i(X, F\otimes L^{\otimes m})$$
214	  %  where the dimensions of the cohomologies are finite because of the
215	  %  coherence and properness conditions."
216	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5); cf.\ [Hartshorne],
217	  I.7.}
218	  Let \(\mathcal{F}_s\) be a coherent sheaf on the fibre \(X_s\) and \(L_s\) a
219	  line bundle on \(X_s\). The \emph{section graded module} of \(\mathcal{F}_s\)
220	  twisted by \(L_s\) is the graded \(R(X_s, L_s)\)-module
221	  (\cref{def:sectionGradedRing})
222	  \[
223	    M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)
224	    \;=\; \bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma\bigl(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}\bigr),
225	  \]
226	  with degree-\(m\) component the \(\kappa(s)\)-vector space
227	  \(\Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\) and scalar action given
228	  by the section tensor product
229	  \(\Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m}) \otimes_{\kappa(s)}
230	  \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m'})
231	  \to \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes (m+m')})\). Its graded Hilbert
232	  function is \(m \mapsto \dim_{\kappa(s)}
233	  \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\).
234	\end{definition}

236	\begin{lemma}
237	[Finite generation of the section graded module]
238	  \label{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg}
239	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.sectionGradedModule_fg}
240	  \uses{def:sectionGradedModule, def:sectionGradedRing}
241	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
242	  % Polynomials" (read from
243	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L453-L464).
244	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
245	  % "Recall that if $F$ is a coherent sheaf on
246	  %  $X$ whose support is proper over $k$, then
247	  %  the \textbf{Hilbert polynomial} ${\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]$ of $F$ is defined by
248	  %  the function
249	  %  $${\Phi}(m) = \chi(F(m)) =
250	  %  \sum_{i=0}^n (-1)^i \dim_k H^i(X, F\otimes L^{\otimes m})$$
251	  %  where the dimensions of the cohomologies are finite because of the
252	  %  coherence and properness conditions."
253	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5); Serre's theorem on
254	  ample line bundles, [Hartshorne], I.7 / II.5.}
255	  Let \(X_s\) be proper over the field \(\kappa(s)\), let \(L_s\) be an ample line
256	  bundle on \(X_s\), and let \(\mathcal{F}_s\) be a coherent sheaf on \(X_s\).
257	  Then the section graded ring \(R(X_s, L_s)\) (\cref{def:sectionGradedRing}) is a
258	  Noetherian graded ring, and the section graded module
259	  \(M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)\) (\cref{def:sectionGradedModule}) is a finitely
260	  generated graded \(R(X_s, L_s)\)-module. In particular each component
261	  \(\Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\) is a finite-dimensional
262	  \(\kappa(s)\)-vector space.
263	\end{lemma}
264	
265	\begin{proof}
266	  \uses{def:sectionGradedRing, def:sectionGradedModule}
267	  Since \(L_s\) is ample and \(X_s\) is proper over \(\kappa(s)\), a Veronese
268	  power \(L_s^{\otimes e}\) is very ample and gives a closed immersion
269	  \(X_s \hookrightarrow \mathbb{P}^N_{\kappa(s)}\). By Serre's theorem the section
270	  graded ring \(R(X_s, L_s)\) is a finitely generated graded
271	  \(\kappa(s)\)-algebra, so it is Noetherian. For the coherent sheaf
272	  \(\mathcal{F}_s\), the same ampleness yields, again by Serre, that
273	  \(\bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\) is a
274	  finitely generated graded module over \(R(X_s, L_s)\): the twists
275	  \(\mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}\) are globally generated for
276	  \(m \gg 0\), and the finitely many generating sections in low degrees together
277	  with the multiplication maps generate \(M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)\) over
278	  \(R(X_s, L_s)\). Finite dimensionality of each component over \(\kappa(s)\)
279	  follows from coherence and properness (finiteness of cohomology of coherent
280	  sheaves on a proper scheme over a field).
281	\end{proof}

283	\begin{lemma}[Existence and uniqueness of the Hilbert polynomial of a rational
284	  graded series]
285	  \label{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}
286	  \lean{Polynomial.existsUnique_hilbertPoly}
287	  \mathlibok
288	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
289	  (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Polynomial.HilbertPoly}).}
290	  Let \(F\) be a field of characteristic zero, let \(p \in F[X]\), and let
291	  \(d \in \mathbb{N}\). Then there is a unique polynomial \(h \in F[X]\) such that
292	  for some \(N \in \mathbb{N}\) and all \(n > N\) the coefficient of \(X^n\) in the
293	  power-series expansion of \(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d} \in F\llbracket X\rrbracket\)
294	  equals \(h(n)\):
295	  \[
296	    \exists!\, h \in F[X],\ \exists N,\ \forall n > N : \;
297	    \bigl[ X^n \bigr]\bigl( p \cdot (1-X)^{-d} \bigr) \;=\; h(n).
298	  \]
299	  Equivalently, the eventual coefficient function of any rational power series
300	  with denominator a power of \(1 - X\) is, for \(n \gg 0\), given by a unique
301	  polynomial \(h\) (Mathlib's \(\mathrm{hilbertPoly}\,p\,d\)). The hypothesis
302	  \([\mathrm{CharZero}\,F]\) is satisfied by \(F = \mathbb{Q}\), which is the
303	  instance used to obtain \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s} \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\).
304	\end{lemma}

306	\begin{lemma}[Additivity of \(\dim_\kappa\) on a short exact sequence
307	  (rank--nullity)]
308	  \label{lem:finrank_ses_additive_mathlib}
309	  \lean{Submodule.finrank_quotient_add_finrank}
310	  \mathlibok
311	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
312	  (\texttt{Mathlib.LinearAlgebra.Dimension.RankNullity}).}
313	  Let \(\kappa\) be a field, \(V\) a finite-dimensional \(\kappa\)-vector space and
314	  \(W \subseteq V\) a subspace. Then
315	  \[
316	    \dim_\kappa (V / W) + \dim_\kappa W \;=\; \dim_\kappa V .
317	  \]
318	  Equivalently, \(\dim_\kappa = \operatorname{finrank}_\kappa\) is additive on a
319	  short exact sequence \(0 \to W \to V \to V/W \to 0\) of finite-dimensional
320	  \(\kappa\)-vector spaces. This is the additivity that converts the degreewise
321	  short exact sequences of graded components into additive relations among the
322	  coefficients of the Hilbert series.
323	\end{lemma}

325	\begin{lemma}[The rational power series \((1 - X)^{-d}\)]
326	  \label{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}
327	  \lean{PowerSeries.invOneSubPow}
328	  \mathlibok
329	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
330	  (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PowerSeries.WellKnown}).}
331	  Let \(S\) be a commutative ring and \(d \in \mathbb{N}\). Then \((1 - X)^d\) is a

333	  inverse
334	  \[
335	    \operatorname{invOneSubPow}(S, d) \;=\; (1 - X)^{-d}
336	      \;\in\; S\llbracket X\rrbracket^{\times}.
337	  \]

339	  \(\operatorname{toPowerSeries} : F[X] \to F\llbracket X\rrbracket\) of a
340	  polynomial to its power series, this is the data in which a rational Hilbert

342	  whose eventual coefficients are extracted by

396	
397	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: [Stacks Project], Tag 00K1, proof, inductive step (read
398	% from references/hilbert-serre-algebra.tex, L13938-L13947).
399	% "Let $\overline{M} = M/xM$. Note that the map $x : M \to M$
400	%  is {\it not} a map of graded $S$-modules, since it does
401	%  not map $M_d$ into $M_d$. Namely, for each $d$ we have the
402	%  following short exact sequence
403	%  $$
404	%  0 \to M_d \xrightarrow{x} M_{d + 1} \to \overline{M}_{d + 1} \to 0
405	%  $$
406	%  This proves that $[M_{d + 1}] - [M_d] = [\overline{M}_{d + 1}]$.
407	%  Hence we win by Lemma \ref{lemma-numerical-polynomial}."
408	\begin{proof}
409	  \uses{lem:finrank_ses_additive_mathlib, lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib,
410	    def:ratHilb, lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero, lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq,
411	    lem:ratHilb_bump, lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_decomposition,
412	    lem:graded_quotient_decomposition, lem:graded_quotientRing_gradedRing,
413	    lem:graded_regrade_over_quotient, lem:graded_finite_transfer,
414	    lem:graded_degreewise_finrank_diff}
415	  We argue with the abstract graded \(\kappa\)-algebra
416	  \(R = \bigoplus_{n \ge 0} R_n\), whose degree-zero part \(R_0 = \kappa\) is a
417	  field, and the finitely generated graded \(R\)-module
418	  \(M = \bigoplus_{n \ge 0} M_n\) with finite-dimensional components \(M_n\);
419	  there is no appeal to any geometric realisation of \(R\) or \(M\).
420	
421	  \emph{Reduction to generation in degree \(1\).} Since \(R\) is Noetherian and
422	  finitely generated as a \(\kappa\)-algebra, it is generated by finitely many
423	  homogeneous elements of positive degree. Replacing \(R\) by a Veronese
424	  subalgebra \(R^{(e)} = \bigoplus_n R_{en}\) for a suitable \(e\) and re-grading
425	  (and \(M\) by the corresponding Veronese module), we may assume \(R\) is
426	  generated by its degree-one part, \(\kappa[R_1] = R\). Then \(R\) is a graded
427	  quotient of a polynomial ring \(\kappa[x_1, \dots, x_r]\) with the \(x_i\) of
428	  degree \(1\); we induct on the minimal number \(r\) of degree-one generators.
429	  % NOTE: The existence of finitely many degree-one generators (the number r the
430	  % induction is on) is expected to follow from the standing hypotheses --
431	  % "finitely generated κ-algebra" together with "generated in degree 1" -- so no
432	  % new hypothesis on gradedModule_hilbertSeries_rational is anticipated. To be
433	  % confirmed when the prover formalizes the G-assembly: degree-1 generation gives
434	  % R₁ as a finite-dimensional κ-vector space spanning R as a κ-algebra, and a
435	  % κ-basis of R₁ is a finite degree-one generating set.
436	
437	  \emph{Base case \(r = 0\).} Here \(R = \kappa\) and \(M\) is a finitely
438	  generated \(\kappa\)-module, hence finite-dimensional, so \(M_n = 0\) and
439	  \(\dim_\kappa M_n = 0\) for all \(n \gg 0\). The Hilbert series
440	  \(\sum_n (\dim_\kappa M_n) X^n\) is therefore a polynomial; taking \(d = 0\)
441	  and \(p \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) the polynomial with these coefficients gives
442	  \(\dim_\kappa M_n = [X^n]\bigl(p \cdot (1 - X)^{0}\bigr)\) for all \(n\).
443	
444	  \emph{Inductive step.} Let \(x = x_r \in R_1\) be one of a minimal set of
445	  degree-one generators. The quotient ring \(R/(x)\) is itself a graded quotient
446	  of \(\kappa[x_1, \dots, x_{r-1}]\), hence an \((r-1)\)-generated graded
447	  \(\kappa\)-algebra to which the induction hypothesis applies; that \(R/(x)\) is a
448	  graded ring with degree-zero part \(\kappa\) is the content of \(G_3\)
449	  (\cref{lem:graded_quotientRing_gradedRing}), using that \((x)\) is homogeneous
450	  (\cref{lem:ideal_homogeneous_span_mathlib}).
451	
452	  \emph{Manufacturing \(K\) and \(C\) as graded \(R/(x)\)-modules.} Put
453	  \(K = \ker\bigl(x : M \to M(1)\bigr)\) and \(C = M/xM\). By \(G_1\)
454	  (\cref{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_decomposition}) \(K\) is an internally
455	  graded \(R\)-module with components \(K_n = \ker(x : M_n \to M_{n+1})\), and by
456	  \(G_2\) (\cref{lem:graded_quotient_decomposition}) \(C = \mathrm{QuotSMulTop}\,
457	  x\,M\) is an internally graded \(R\)-module with components
458	  \(C_n = M_n/x M_{n-1}\). Both are annihilated by \(x\), so by \(G_4\)
459	  (\cref{lem:graded_regrade_over_quotient}) their action descends to \(R/(x)\) with
460	  the same component families, and by \(G_5\)
461	  (\cref{lem:graded_finite_transfer}) they are finitely generated graded
462	  \(R/(x)\)-modules (\(C\) a quotient of the finitely generated \(M\), \(K\) a
463	  submodule of \(M\) over the Noetherian \(R\)). Writing
464	  \(h_M(n) = \dim_\kappa M_n\), \(h_C(n) = \dim_\kappa C_n\),
465	  \(h_K(n) = \dim_\kappa K_n\), the induction hypothesis applied to the
466	  \(R/(x)\)-modules \(C\) and \(K\) yields a common order \(d\) (after
467	  \cref{lem:ratHilb_bump} raises the lower of the two orders) with
468	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_C, d)\) and \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_K, d)\)
469	  (\cref{def:ratHilb}).
470	
471	  \emph{The difference identity.} Multiplication by \(x\) is the \(\kappa\)-linear
472	  map \(x : M_n \to M_{n+1}\); applying the degreewise rank--nullity identity
473	  \(D_5\) (\cref{lem:graded_degreewise_finrank_diff}) with \(V = M_n\),
474	  \(W = M_{n+1}\) gives, for every \(n\),
475	  \[
476	    h_M(n+1) - h_M(n)
477	      \;=\; \dim_\kappa M_{n+1} - \dim_\kappa M_n
478	      \;=\; \dim_\kappa C_{n+1} - \dim_\kappa K_n
479	      \;=\; h_C(n+1) - h_K(n),
480	  \]
481	  using the identifications \(\ker(x : M_n \to M_{n+1}) = K_n\) and
482	  \(M_{n+1}/x M_n = C_{n+1}\) from \(G_1\), \(G_2\). This is exactly the
483	  \(\mathrm{hdiff}\) hypothesis of the inductive-step engine.
484	
485	  \emph{Conclusion.} Feeding \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_C, d)\),
486	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_K, d)\) and the difference identity into
487	  \cref{lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq} yields \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_M, d+1)\): there are
488	  \(p \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\), \(d+1 \in \mathbb{N}\) and \(N \in \mathbb{N}\) with
489	  \(\dim_\kappa M_n = [X^n]\bigl(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-(d+1)}\bigr)\) for all
490	  \(n > N\), which is the claim (with pole order \(d+1 \le r\)).
491	\end{proof}

493	\subsection{The rationality toolkit \texorpdfstring{\(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}\)}{IsRatHilb}}
494	\label{subsec:isRatHilb}
495	
496	The proof of \cref{lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational} runs the Stacks~00K1 induction
497	on the number of degree-one generators. Each inductive step manipulates the
498	\emph{shape} of the Hilbert series -- a numerator polynomial over a power of
499	\((1-X)\) -- rather than the module itself. We isolate that bookkeeping in a
500	single predicate \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}\) on functions \(f : \mathbb{N} \to
501	\mathbb{Q}\), together with the closure lemmas the induction consumes. These are
502	project-internal helpers realising standard steps of the Stacks~00K1 argument at
503	the level of power series; Mathlib supplies only the converse extraction
504	(\cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}).
505	
506	\begin{lemma}[Coefficients of \((1-X)^{-1}\) times a series are partial sums]
507	  \label{lem:coeff_invOneSubPow_one_mul}
508	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.coeff_invOneSubPow_one_mul}
509	  \uses{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}
510	  Let \(F \in \mathbb{Q}\llbracket X\rrbracket\) be a power series. Then for every
511	  \(n \in \mathbb{N}\) the \(n\)-th coefficient of
512	  \(\operatorname{invOneSubPow}(\mathbb{Q}, 1)\cdot F = (1-X)^{-1}\cdot F\)
513	  (\cref{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}) is the partial sum of the coefficients of
514	  \(F\):
515	  \[
516	    \bigl[X^n\bigr]\bigl((1-X)^{-1}\cdot F\bigr)
517	    \;=\; \sum_{k=0}^{n} \bigl[X^k\bigr] F .
518	  \]
519	  Equivalently, multiplication by \((1-X)^{-1} = \sum_{m\ge 0} X^m\) is the
520	  summation operator on coefficient sequences. Proved directly in Lean from the
521	  identity \(\operatorname{invOneSubPow}(\mathbb{Q},1) = \sum_{m\ge 0} X^m\) and
522	  the Cauchy product.
523	\end{lemma}
524	
525	\begin{lemma}[Antidifference of a rational Hilbert series]
526	  \label{lem:rationalHilbert_antidiff}
527	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.rationalHilbert_antidiff}
528	  \uses{lem:coeff_invOneSubPow_one_mul, lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}
529	  Let \(H, \delta : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{Q}\), let \(q \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) and
530	  \(e, N \in \mathbb{N}\). Suppose that for all \(n > N\) the sequence \(\delta\)
531	  is the coefficient sequence of the rational series \(q\cdot(1-X)^{-e}\),
532	  \[
533	    \delta(n) \;=\; \bigl[X^n\bigr]\bigl(q\cdot(1-X)^{-e}\bigr),
534	  \]
535	  and that \(\delta\) is the first difference of \(H\) beyond \(N\), i.e.
536	  \(H(n+1) - H(n) = \delta(n+1)\) for all \(n > N\). Then there is a numerator
537	  polynomial \(p \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) such that for all \(n > N\)
538	  \[
539	    H(n) \;=\; \bigl[X^n\bigr]\bigl(p\cdot(1-X)^{-(e+1)}\bigr).
540	  \]
541	  That is, summing a coefficient sequence (anti-differencing) raises the pole
542	  order by one: the partial-sum series of \(q\cdot(1-X)^{-e}\) equals
543	  \(p\cdot(1-X)^{-(e+1)}\) up to a polynomial correction in low degrees. This is
544	  the power-series heart of the Stacks~00K1 inductive step.
545	\end{lemma}
546	
547	\begin{proof}
548	  \uses{lem:coeff_invOneSubPow_one_mul, lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}
549	  Write \(F = q\cdot(1-X)^{-e}\). Multiplying by
550	  \((1-X)^{-1}\) and using the factorisation
551	  \((1-X)^{-(e+1)} = (1-X)^{-1}\cdot(1-X)^{-e}\)
552	  (\cref{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}), \cref{lem:coeff_invOneSubPow_one_mul} gives
553	  \(\bigl[X^m\bigr]\bigl(q\cdot(1-X)^{-(e+1)}\bigr) = \sum_{k=0}^{m}[X^k]F\), the
554	  partial sums of \(F\). On the other hand, telescoping the first-difference
555	  hypothesis from \(N+1\) expresses \(H(N+1+j)\) as \(H(N+1)\) plus a window sum
556	  of the coefficients of \(F\). Splitting the partial sum
557	  \(\sum_{k=0}^{n}[X^k]F\) at \(N+2\) and absorbing the resulting constant
558	  discrepancy into a numerator \(C\,(1-X)^{e}\) (a constant function is the
559	  order-\((e+1)\) coefficient sequence of \(C\,(1-X)^{e}\cdot(1-X)^{-(e+1)} =
560	  C\,(1-X)^{-1}\)), the explicit numerator
561	  \(p = C\,(1-X)^{e} + q\) realises \(H(n)\) as
562	  \([X^n]\bigl(p\cdot(1-X)^{-(e+1)}\bigr)\) for all \(n > N\).
563	\end{proof}
564	
565	\begin{definition}[Rational Hilbert function]
566	  \label{def:ratHilb}
567	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsRatHilb}
568	  \uses{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}
569	  \textit{Source: Stacks 00K1.}
570	  For a function \(f : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{Q}\) and \(d \in \mathbb{N}\), say
571	  that \(f\) is a \emph{rational Hilbert function of order \(d\)}, written
572	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(f, d)\), when there exist a numerator polynomial
573	  \(p \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) and an \(N \in \mathbb{N}\) such that for all \(n > N\)
574	  \[
575	    f(n) \;=\; \bigl[X^n\bigr]\bigl(p\cdot(1-X)^{-d}\bigr)
576	    \;=\; \bigl[X^n\bigr]\bigl(\operatorname{toPowerSeries}(p)\cdot
577	      \operatorname{invOneSubPow}(\mathbb{Q}, d)\bigr)
578	  \]
579	  (\cref{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}). This is the ``eventually equal to the
580	  coefficient sequence of a rational series \(p\cdot(1-X)^{-d}\)'' predicate that
581	  the graded Hilbert--Serre induction tracks; its conclusion shape is exactly the
582	  one consumed by \cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}.
583	\end{definition}
584	
585	\begin{lemma}[Base case: eventually-zero functions are rational of order \(0\)]
586	  \label{lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero}
587	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsRatHilb.ofEventuallyZero}
588	  \uses{def:ratHilb}
589	  If \(f : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{Q}\) is eventually zero -- there is
590	  \(N\) with \(f(n) = 0\) for all \(n > N\) -- then \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(f, 0)\)
591	  (\cref{def:ratHilb}). Indeed \(f\) is then the coefficient sequence of the
592	  numerator polynomial \(0\) over \((1-X)^{0} = 1\). This is the base case of the
593	  Stacks~00K1 induction (no degree-one generators). Proved directly in Lean.
594	\end{lemma}
595	
596	\begin{lemma}[Raising the pole order]
597	  \label{lem:ratHilb_bump}
598	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsRatHilb.bump}
599	  \uses{def:ratHilb, lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}
600	  If \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(f, d)\) (\cref{def:ratHilb}) then
601	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(f, d+1)\): multiplying the numerator by \((1-X)\) cancels
602	  one factor of \((1-X)^{-1}\), so the same coefficient sequence is realised at
603	  pole order \(d+1\). This lets two rational Hilbert functions of different
604	  orders be brought to a common denominator. Proved directly in Lean using
605	  \((1-X)\cdot(1-X)^{-(d+1)} = (1-X)^{-d}\) (\cref{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}).
606	\end{lemma}
607	
608	\begin{lemma}[Closure under difference]
609	  \label{lem:ratHilb_sub}
610	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsRatHilb.sub}
611	  \uses{def:ratHilb}
612	  If \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(f, d)\) and \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(g, d)\)
613	  (\cref{def:ratHilb}) at the same order \(d\), then
614	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(n \mapsto f(n) - g(n),\, d)\): the numerator of the
615	  difference is the difference of the numerators (over the common denominator
616	  \((1-X)^{-d}\)). Proved directly in Lean.
617	\end{lemma}
618	
619	\begin{lemma}[Closure under right shift]
620	  \label{lem:ratHilb_shiftRight}
621	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsRatHilb.shiftRight}
622	  \uses{def:ratHilb}
623	  If \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(f, d)\) (\cref{def:ratHilb}) then
624	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(n \mapsto f(n-1),\, d)\): the right shift on coefficient
625	  sequences is multiplication of the series by \(X\), so the numerator
626	  \(p\) is replaced by \(X\cdot p\) at the same pole order. Proved directly in
627	  Lean.
628	\end{lemma}
629	
630	\begin{lemma}[Antidifference step for the predicate]
631	  \label{lem:ratHilb_antidiff}
632	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsRatHilb.antidiff}
633	  \uses{def:ratHilb, lem:rationalHilbert_antidiff}
634	  Let \(H, g : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{Q}\) and \(e, N \in \mathbb{N}\). If
635	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(g, e)\) (\cref{def:ratHilb}) and \(g\) is the first
636	  difference of \(H\) beyond \(N\) (\(H(n+1) - H(n) = g(n+1)\) for all
637	  \(n > N\)), then \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(H, e+1)\). This is the predicate-level
638	  packaging of \cref{lem:rationalHilbert_antidiff}: a finite difference raises
639	  the pole order by one, the inductive heart of graded Hilbert--Serre.
640	\end{lemma}
641	
642	\begin{proof}
643	  \uses{def:ratHilb, lem:rationalHilbert_antidiff}
644	  Unfolding \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(g, e)\) supplies a numerator \(q\) and a
645	  threshold \(N_g\) with \(g(n) = [X^n](q\cdot(1-X)^{-e})\) for \(n > N_g\).
646	  Applying \cref{lem:rationalHilbert_antidiff} with this \(q\) and threshold
647	  \(\max(N, N_g)\) -- whose two hypotheses are exactly the rational form of \(g\)
648	  and the first-difference identity for \(H\) -- yields a numerator \(p\) with
649	  \(H(n) = [X^n](p\cdot(1-X)^{-(e+1)})\) for \(n > \max(N, N_g)\), i.e.
650	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(H, e+1)\).
651	\end{proof}
652	
653	\begin{lemma}[Inductive-step engine from a degreewise short exact sequence]
654	  \label{lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq}
655	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsRatHilb.ofDiffEq}
656	  \uses{def:ratHilb, lem:ratHilb_antidiff, lem:ratHilb_sub, lem:ratHilb_shiftRight}
657	  Let \(h_M, h_C, h_K : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{Q}\) and \(d, N \in \mathbb{N}\).
658	  Suppose \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_C, d)\) and \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_K, d)\)
659	  (\cref{def:ratHilb}), and that for all \(n > N\)
660	  \[
661	    h_M(n+1) - h_M(n) \;=\; h_C(n+1) - h_K(n).
662	  \]
663	  Then \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_M, d+1)\). This packages the entire power-series
664	  side of the Stacks~00K1 inductive step: \(h_M\) is the Hilbert function of
665	  \(M\), and \(h_C, h_K\) are the Hilbert functions of the cokernel
666	  \(C = M/xM\) and kernel \(K = \ker(x : M \to M(1))\) of multiplication by a
667	  degree-one element \(x\); the displayed identity is the alternating-sum
668	  consequence of the degreewise short exact sequence
669	  \(0 \to K_n \to M_n \xrightarrow{x} M_{n+1} \to C_{n+1} \to 0\). Given the
670	  inductive hypothesis that \(h_C, h_K\) are rational of order \(d\), the
671	  conclusion is rationality of \(h_M\) at order \(d+1\).
672	\end{lemma}
673	
674	\begin{proof}
675	  \uses{lem:ratHilb_antidiff, lem:ratHilb_sub, lem:ratHilb_shiftRight}
676	  Set \(g(n) = h_C(n) - h_K(n-1)\). By closure under right shift
677	  (\cref{lem:ratHilb_shiftRight}) \(n \mapsto h_K(n-1)\) is rational of order
678	  \(d\), and by closure under difference (\cref{lem:ratHilb_sub}) so is \(g\). The
679	  hypothesis rewrites as \(h_M(n+1) - h_M(n) = g(n+1)\) for \(n > N\) (since
680	  \(g(n+1) = h_C(n+1) - h_K(n)\)). Applying the antidifference step
681	  (\cref{lem:ratHilb_antidiff}) to \(H = h_M\) and this \(g\) gives
682	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_M, d+1)\).
683	\end{proof}

685	\begin{theorem}[Hilbert polynomial from the section module]
686	  \label{thm:hilbertPoly_of_sectionModule}
687	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.hilbertPolynomialOfSectionModule}
688	  \uses{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg, lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational,
689	    lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}
690	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
691	  % Polynomials" (read from
692	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L468-L478).
693	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
694	  % "Let $X\to S$ be a finite type morphism of noetherian schemes,
695	  %  and let $L$ be a line bundle on $X$. Let $\F$
696	  %  be any coherent sheaf on
697	  %  $X$ whose schematic support is proper over $S$. Then for each $s\in S$,
698	  %  we get a polynomial ${\Phi}_s \in  \Q[\lambda]$
699	  %  which is the Hilbert polynomial of
700	  %  the restriction $\F_s = F|_{X_s}$ of $\F$ to the fiber $X_s$ over $s$,
701	  %  calculated with respect to the line bundle $L_s = L|_{X_s}$."
702	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
703	  Let \(X_s\) be proper over the field \(\kappa(s)\), let \(L_s\) be an ample line
704	  bundle on \(X_s\), and let \(\mathcal{F}_s\) be a coherent sheaf on \(X_s\).
705	  Then there is a unique polynomial \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s} \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\)
706	  such that for all \(m \gg 0\)
707	  \[
708	    \Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}(m)
709	    \;=\; \dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma\bigl(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}\bigr).
710	  \]
711	  This \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\) is the Hilbert polynomial of
712	  \cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}.
713	\end{theorem}
714	
715	\begin{proof}
716	  \uses{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg, lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational,
717	    lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}
718	  By \cref{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg} the section graded module
719	  \(M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)\) is a finitely generated graded module over the
720	  Noetherian graded ring \(R(X_s, L_s)\), with finite-dimensional components and
721	  degree-zero part the field \(\kappa(s)\). Applying the abstract graded
722	  Hilbert--Serre rationality lemma (\cref{lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational}) to the
723	  graded \(\kappa(s)\)-algebra \(R = R(X_s, L_s)\) and the finitely generated
724	  graded module \(M = M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)\) exhibits the graded Hilbert
725	  function
726	  \(m \mapsto \dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\)
727	  as the eventual coefficient function of a rational series: there are
728	  \(p \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) and \(d \in \mathbb{N}\) and an \(N\) with
729	  \(\dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})
730	  = [X^m]\,(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d})\) for \(m > N\). Apply
731	  \cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib} over the characteristic-zero field
732	  \(F = \mathbb{Q}\) to this pair \((p, d)\): it yields a unique polynomial
733	  \(h \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) whose values \(h(n)\) eventually equal
734	  \([X^n]\,(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d})\). Setting
735	  \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s} := h\) (with the variable renamed \(\lambda\)) gives a
736	  polynomial with \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}(m) = \dim_{\kappa(s)}
737	  \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\) for \(m \gg 0\). Uniqueness
738	  is the \(\exists!\) clause of \cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}: any two
739	  polynomials agreeing with the Hilbert function for \(m \gg 0\) agree at
740	  infinitely many points, hence are equal.
741	\end{proof}

743	\subsection{The graded-module wrapper for the Stacks~00K1 inductive step}
744	\label{subsec:gradedModuleApi}
745	
746	The power-series side of \cref{lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational} is fully isolated
747	in the \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}\) toolkit (\cref{subsec:isRatHilb}). What remains is
748	the \emph{graded-module} side of the Stacks~00K1 induction: to apply the
749	inductive hypothesis to the kernel and cokernel of multiplication by a degree-one
750	element, those two modules must be presented as finitely generated graded modules
751	over the smaller ring \(R/(x)\). This subsection records the small wrapper that
752	manufactures that presentation. It is a thin layer over existing Mathlib scaffold:
753	Mathlib already provides the notion of a homogeneous submodule
754	(\cref{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}), the quotient \(M/xM\) as a named
755	object (\cref{lem:quotSMulTop_mathlib}), the homogeneity of a span of homogeneous
756	elements (\cref{lem:ideal_homogeneous_span_mathlib}), the rank--nullity identity
757	(\cref{lem:finrank_range_add_finrank_ker_mathlib}), and the finite-generation
758	transfer along a surjection (\cref{lem:fg_restrictScalars_of_surjective_mathlib});
759	what is missing -- and supplied here -- is the \emph{induced grading} on the
760	derived objects (a homogeneous submodule, a homogeneous quotient module, the
761	quotient ring \(R/(x)\)) together with the regrading and finite-module transfer
762	over \(R/(x)\). Each block mirrors the shape of Mathlib's \(\mathrm{HomogeneousIdeal}\)
763	/ \(\mathrm{GradedRing}\) API: an unbundled \(\mathrm{DirectSum.Decomposition}\)
764	instance on a family \(n \mapsto (\cdot)\) of submodules, together with a
765	\(\mathrm{SetLike.GradedSMul}\) instance for the action.
766	
767	\emph{Setting for this subsection.} Let \(R = \bigoplus_{n \ge 0} R_n\) be a graded
768	ring whose degree-zero part \(R_0 = \kappa\) is a field and which is generated as a
769	\(\kappa\)-algebra in degree one, and let \(M = \bigoplus_{n \ge 0} M_n\) be a
770	finitely generated graded \(R\)-module with finite-dimensional components \(M_n\).
771	Fix a homogeneous element \(x \in R_1\). Write \(K = \ker\bigl(x : M \to M(1)\bigr)\)
772	for the graded kernel of multiplication by \(x\) and \(C = M/xM\) for the cokernel.
773	
774	\emph{Build order.} The blocks are most cheaply discharged in the order
775	\(G_1 \to G_2 \to G_5 \to G_3 \to G_4 \to\) assembly: \(G_1\), \(G_2\), \(G_5\)
776	carry the least risk and unblock the most, while the degreewise rank--nullity
777	identity \(D_5\) (\cref{lem:graded_degreewise_finrank_diff}) is pure linear algebra
778	and can be discharged independently of all the graded blocks.
779	
780	\subsubsection*{Mathlib dependency anchors}
781	
782	\begin{lemma}[Homogeneous submodule]
783	  \label{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}
784	  \lean{Submodule.IsHomogeneous}
785	  \mathlibok
786	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
787	  (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.GradedAlgebra.Homogeneous.Submodule}).}
788	  Let \(R = \bigoplus_n R_n\) be a graded ring and \(M = \bigoplus_n M_n\) an
789	  internally graded \(R\)-module. A submodule \(p \subseteq M\) is
790	  \emph{homogeneous} (\(p.\mathrm{IsHomogeneous}\,\mathcal{M}\)) when it is closed
791	  under taking homogeneous components: \(m \in p\) implies each graded piece

872	\begin{lemma}[$G_1$ -- grading on a homogeneous submodule]
873	  \label{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_decomposition}
874	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.homogeneousSubmodule_decomposition}
875	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib, lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}
876	  Let \(M = \bigoplus_n M_n\) be an internally graded \(R\)-module and let
877	  \(p \subseteq M\) be a homogeneous submodule
878	  (\cref{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}). Then the family
879	  \(n \mapsto p \cap M_n\) of \(\kappa\)-subspaces carries a
880	  \(\mathrm{DirectSum.Decomposition}\) -- i.e. \(p = \bigoplus_n (p \cap M_n)\)
881	  internally -- together with a \(\mathrm{SetLike.GradedSMul}\,\mathcal{A}\)
882	  instance for the \(R\)-action restricted to \(p\). In particular the graded
883	  kernel \(K = \ker(x : M \to M(1))\), which is a homogeneous submodule (an element
884	  \(m = \sum_n m_n\) satisfies \(x m = 0\) iff each \(x m_n = 0\), because the
885	  \(x m_n\) lie in distinct degrees), inherits the grading
886	  \(K_n = K \cap M_n = \ker(x : M_n \to M_{n+1})\).
887	\end{lemma}
888	
889	\begin{proof}
890	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib, lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}
891	  The internal decomposition of \(M\) restricts to \(p\). For \(m \in p\), write
892	  \(m = \sum_n \mathrm{proj}_n(m)\) with \(\mathrm{proj}_n(m) \in M_n\) (the
893	  decomposition of \(M\)); homogeneity of \(p\) gives \(\mathrm{proj}_n(m) \in p\),
894	  hence \(\mathrm{proj}_n(m) \in p \cap M_n\). This exhibits every element of \(p\)
895	  as a finite sum of elements of the subspaces \(p \cap M_n\), so the canonical map
896	  \(\bigoplus_n (p \cap M_n) \to p\) is surjective; it is injective because the
897	  \(M_n\) are independent in \(M\). Thus the family is a
898	  \(\mathrm{DirectSum.Decomposition}\). For the graded action: if \(a \in R_i\) and
899	  \(m \in p \cap M_n\), then \(a m \in M_{i+n}\) by
900	  \cref{lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib} and \(a m \in p\) because \(p\)
901	  is a submodule, so \(a m \in p \cap M_{i+n}\), which is the
902	  \(\mathrm{SetLike.GradedSMul}\) condition. Finally \(K\) is homogeneous: the
903	  components \(x m_n \in M_{n+1}\) of \(x m\) lie in distinct degrees, so
904	  \(x m = 0\) forces each \(x m_n = 0\); thus \(K \cap M_n = \ker(x : M_n \to
905	  M_{n+1})\).
906	\end{proof}

908	\begin{lemma}[$G_2$ -- grading on the quotient by a homogeneous submodule]
909	  \label{lem:graded_quotient_decomposition}
910	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.quotient_decomposition}
911	  \uses{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_decomposition, lem:quotSMulTop_mathlib,
912	    lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}
913	  Let \(p \subseteq M\) be a homogeneous submodule. Then the quotient module
914	  \(M/p\) carries a \(\mathrm{DirectSum.Decomposition}\) with components the images
915	  \((M_n).\mathrm{map}\,(p.\mathrm{mkQ})\) of the graded pieces under the quotient
916	  map \(p.\mathrm{mkQ} : M \to M/p\), together with a compatible
917	  \(\mathrm{SetLike.GradedSMul}\,\mathcal{A}\) instance. Specialising to
918	  \(p = xM = x \cdot (\top)\) gives the induced grading on
919	  \(C = M/xM = \mathrm{QuotSMulTop}\,x\,M\)
920	  (\cref{lem:quotSMulTop_mathlib}), with components
921	  \(C_{n} = (M_{n}).\mathrm{map}\,(\mathrm{mkQ})\).
922	\end{lemma}

925	  \uses{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_decomposition, lem:quotSMulTop_mathlib,
926	    lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}
927	  Surjectivity of \(\bigoplus_n (M_n).\mathrm{map}\,(p.\mathrm{mkQ}) \to M/p\) is
928	  immediate: \(p.\mathrm{mkQ}\) is surjective and \(M = \bigoplus_n M_n\), so every
929	  class \([m]\) is a finite sum of classes \([m_n]\) with \(m_n \in M_n\). For
930	  independence, suppose \(\sum_n [m_n] = 0\) in \(M/p\) with \(m_n \in M_n\). Then
931	  \(\sum_n m_n \in p\); by \(G_1\)
932	  (\cref{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_decomposition}) \(p =
933	  \bigoplus_n (p \cap M_n)\), so taking degree-\(n\) components gives
934	  \(m_n \in p \cap M_n\), i.e. \([m_n] = 0\) for every \(n\). Hence the components
935	  \((M_n).\mathrm{map}\,(p.\mathrm{mkQ})\) are independent and the family is a
936	  \(\mathrm{DirectSum.Decomposition}\) of \(M/p\). Concretely this realises
937	  \(M/p \cong \bigoplus_n M_n/(p \cap M_n)\). The graded action descends: for
938	  \(a \in R_i\) and a class \([m]\) with \(m \in M_n\), \(a \cdot [m] = [a m]\) and
939	  \(a m \in M_{i+n}\) by \cref{lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}, so the
940	  action lands in component \(i+n\); this is the \(\mathrm{SetLike.GradedSMul}\)
941	  instance. Taking \(p = xM\) -- homogeneous with degree-\((n+1)\) part
942	  \(x \cdot M_n\) by \cref{lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib} -- yields the

944	\end{proof}

1042	\begin{lemma}[$D_5$ -- degreewise rank--nullity difference]
1043	  \label{lem:graded_degreewise_finrank_diff}
1044	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.degreewise_finrank_diff}
1045	  \uses{lem:finrank_range_add_finrank_ker_mathlib,
1046	    lem:finrank_ses_additive_mathlib}
1047	  Let \(\varphi : V \to W\) be a \(\kappa\)-linear map between finite-dimensional
1048	  \(\kappa\)-vector spaces. Then
1049	  \[
1050	    \dim_\kappa W - \dim_\kappa V
1051	      \;=\; \dim_\kappa(W/\operatorname{range}\varphi) - \dim_\kappa(\ker\varphi).
1052	  \]
1053	  Applied degreewise with \(\varphi = (x : M_n \to M_{n+1})\), \(V = M_n\),
1054	  \(W = M_{n+1}\) -- so that \(\ker\varphi = K_n\)
1055	  (\cref{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_decomposition}) and
1056	  \(W/\operatorname{range}\varphi = M_{n+1}/x M_n = C_{n+1}\)
1057	  (\cref{lem:graded_quotient_decomposition}, since the degree-\((n+1)\) part of
1058	  \(xM\) is \(x M_n\)) -- this gives, for every \(n\),
1059	  \[
1060	    \dim_\kappa M_{n+1} - \dim_\kappa M_n
1061	      \;=\; \dim_\kappa C_{n+1} - \dim_\kappa K_n,
1062	  \]
1063	  which is the difference identity \(\mathrm{hdiff}\) consumed by
1064	  \cref{lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq}. No graded structure is used in proving the
1065	  underlying vector-space identity -- it is pure rank--nullity.
1066	\end{lemma}
1067	
1068	\begin{proof}
1069	  \uses{lem:finrank_range_add_finrank_ker_mathlib,
1070	    lem:finrank_ses_additive_mathlib}
1071	  By rank--nullity (\cref{lem:finrank_range_add_finrank_ker_mathlib}),
1072	  \(\dim_\kappa V = \dim_\kappa(\ker\varphi) + \dim_\kappa(\operatorname{range}
1073	  \varphi)\), so \(\dim_\kappa(\operatorname{range}\varphi) = \dim_\kappa V -
1074	  \dim_\kappa(\ker\varphi)\). By additivity on the short exact sequence
1075	  \(0 \to \operatorname{range}\varphi \to W \to W/\operatorname{range}\varphi \to 0\)
1076	  (\cref{lem:finrank_ses_additive_mathlib}),
1077	  \(\dim_\kappa(W/\operatorname{range}\varphi) = \dim_\kappa W -
1078	  \dim_\kappa(\operatorname{range}\varphi) = \dim_\kappa W - \dim_\kappa V +
1079	  \dim_\kappa(\ker\varphi)\). Rearranging gives
1080	  \(\dim_\kappa W - \dim_\kappa V = \dim_\kappa(W/\operatorname{range}\varphi) -
1081	  \dim_\kappa(\ker\varphi)\). The degreewise application identifies
1082	  \(\ker(x : M_n \to M_{n+1}) = K_n\) and
1083	  \(M_{n+1}/\operatorname{range}(x : M_n \to M_{n+1}) = C_{n+1}\) through the
1084	  gradings of \(G_1\) and \(G_2\), the latter because the degree-\((n+1)\) graded
1085	  piece of the homogeneous submodule \(xM\) is exactly \(x M_n\)
1086	  (\cref{lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}).
1087	\end{proof}

1089	\section{Support and freeness predicates}
1090	\label{sec:quot_predicates}
1091	
1092	The Quot functor (\cref{def:quot_functor}) and the Grassmannian
1093	(\cref{def:grassmannian_scheme}) classify quotient sheaves subject to two
1094	geometric conditions: a coherent quotient must have \emph{schematic support
1095	proper over the base}, and the Grassmannian's quotients must be \emph{locally
1096	free of a fixed rank}. This section introduces both predicates.

1181	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1182	[Annihilator of a finitely generated module commutes with
1183	  localization]
1184	  \label{lem:annihilator_localization_eq_map}
1185	  \lean{Module.annihilator_isLocalizedModule_eq_map}
1186	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, let \(S \subseteq R\) be a multiplicative
1187	  submonoid, and let \(R_S = S^{-1}R\) be the localization of \(R\) at \(S\). Let
1188	  \(M\) be a \emph{finitely generated} \(R\)-module and let \(f : M \to M_S\) be
1189	  an \(R\)-linear map exhibiting \(M_S\) as the localized module \(S^{-1}M\)
1190	  (i.e.\ \(f\) is a localization of \(M\) at \(S\)). Then the annihilator of
1191	  \(M_S\) over \(R_S\) is the extension of the annihilator of \(M\) along the
1192	  structure map \(R \to R_S\):
1193	  \[
1194	    \mathrm{Ann}_{R_S}(M_S)
1195	    \;=\; (\mathrm{Ann}_R M)\cdot R_S
1196	    \;=\; \mathrm{map}\bigl(R \to R_S\bigr)\bigl(\mathrm{Ann}_R M\bigr).
1197	  \]
1198	  Equivalently, for finitely generated \(M\) the annihilator commutes with
1199	  localization: \(\mathrm{Ann}(S^{-1}M) = S^{-1}\,\mathrm{Ann}(M)\).
1200	
1201	  \emph{The finite-generation hypothesis is essential.} For a module that is not
1202	  finitely generated the inclusion \(S^{-1}\,\mathrm{Ann}(M) \subseteq
1203	  \mathrm{Ann}(S^{-1}M)\) can be strict, so the identity fails. This is the
1204	  standard commutative-algebra fact that the annihilator of a finite module
1205	  localizes; it is recorded here as the load-bearing algebra engine for the
1206	  basic-open coherence of \cref{def:modules_annihilator}.
1207	\end{lemma}
1208	
1209	\begin{proof}
1210	  \(\supseteq\): if \(r \in \mathrm{Ann}_R M\), then \(r\) kills every element of
1211	  \(M\), hence its image \(r/1 \in R_S\) kills every element of \(M_S\) (each such
1212	  element is \(m/s = (1/s)\cdot f(m)\) for some \(m \in M\), \(s \in S\), and
1213	  \((r/1)\cdot(m/s) = (1/s)\cdot f(r\,m) = 0\)). Thus
1214	  \((\mathrm{Ann}_R M)\cdot R_S \subseteq \mathrm{Ann}_{R_S}(M_S)\).
1215	
1216	  \(\subseteq\): write \(M = \langle m_1, \dots, m_n\rangle\) using the
1217	  finite-generation hypothesis, and let \(y \in \mathrm{Ann}_{R_S}(M_S)\). Write
1218	  \(y = a/s\) with \(a \in R\), \(s \in S\). For each generator \(m_i\) the element
1219	  \(y\cdot f(m_i) = (a/s)\cdot f(m_i) = 0\) in \(M_S\); by the definition of the
1220	  localized module this means there is \(u_i \in S\) with \(u_i\,a\,m_i = 0\) in
1221	  \(M\). Put \(u = \prod_{i=1}^n u_i \in S\). Then \(u\,a\) kills every generator
1222	  \(m_i\), hence kills all of \(M\), so \(u\,a \in \mathrm{Ann}_R M\). Finally
1223	  \(y = a/s = (u\,a)/(u\,s)\) lies in \(S^{-1}\,\mathrm{Ann}_R M =
1224	  (\mathrm{Ann}_R M)\cdot R_S\). Therefore \(\mathrm{Ann}_{R_S}(M_S) \subseteq
1225	  (\mathrm{Ann}_R M)\cdot R_S\), and combined with the reverse inclusion the two
1226	  ideals coincide. The use of a single common multiplier \(u = \prod_i u_i\) is
1227	  exactly the step that requires finitely many generators.
1228	\end{proof}

1246	  \label{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}

1255	  %  associated to $M$.
1256	  %  [...]

1260	  \(\widetilde M\) on \(\Spec(R)\), item (4).}
1261	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let \(\mathcal{M}\) be a \emph{quasi-coherent} sheaf
1262	  of modules on \(X\). Let \(U \subseteq X\) be an
1263	  affine open and \(f \in \Gamma(X, U)\). Then:
1264	  \begin{enumerate}
1265	    \item \(\Gamma\bigl(X, D(f)\bigr)\) is the localization \((\Gamma(X,U))_f\)
1266	      of \(\Gamma(X,U)\) away from \(f\)
1267	      (\cref{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}); and
1268	    \item the section-restriction map
1269	      \(\mathcal{M}(U) \to \mathcal{M}\bigl(D(f)\bigr)\) exhibits
1270	      \(\mathcal{M}(D(f))\) as the localized module
1271	      \((\mathrm{powers}\,f)^{-1}\,\mathcal{M}(U)\); that is, it is a localization
1272	      of the \(\Gamma(X,U)\)-module \(\mathcal{M}(U)\) at the submonoid
1273	      \(\mathrm{powers}\,f = \{1, f, f^2, \dots\}\).

1277	\begin{proof}
1278	  \uses{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}
1279	  Part (1) is \cref{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}, the Mathlib statement
1280	  that on an affine open \(U\) the sections over the basic open \(D(f)\) form the
1281	  localization of \(\Gamma(X,U)\) away from \(f\).
1282	
1283	  For part (2), identify the affine open \(U\) with \(\Spec \Gamma(X, U)\). Over
1284	  this affine, a quasi-coherent sheaf is \(\widetilde{N}\) for the
1285	  \(\Gamma(X,U)\)-module \(N = \mathcal{M}(U)\), the sheaf associated to
1286	  \(N\) (no finiteness of \(N\) is required for this step). The Spec-local
1287	  computation of the sections of \(\widetilde{N}\) on a
1288	  basic open gives \(\Gamma\bigl(D(f), \widetilde{N}\bigr) = N_f\) as an
1289	  \((\Gamma(X,U))_f\)-module, and the restriction map
1290	  \(\Gamma(\Spec \Gamma(X,U), \widetilde{N}) = N \to N_f\) is precisely the
1291	  localization homomorphism \(N \to (\mathrm{powers}\,f)^{-1} N\). Transporting
1292	  this identification across the affine isomorphism
1293	  \(U \cong \Spec \Gamma(X, U)\) -- under which \(\mathcal{M}|_U\) corresponds to
1294	  \(\widetilde{N}\) by quasi-coherence and the basic open \(D(f) \subseteq U\)
1295	  corresponds to the standard open \(D(f) \subseteq \Spec\Gamma(X,U)\) -- shows
1296	  that the restriction \(\mathcal{M}(U) \to \mathcal{M}(D(f))\) is the
1297	  localization of \(N = \mathcal{M}(U)\) at \(\mathrm{powers}\,f\), i.e.\ it is a
1298	  localized module at \(\mathrm{powers}\,f\). The substantive content is this
1299	  transport of the Spec-local localization fact for the sheaf \(\widetilde{N}\)
1300	  to a general quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{M}\) via the affine identification.

1650	  
1651	  Over \(S = \Spec \mathbb{Z}\) and \(V = \mathcal{O}_S^{\oplus r}\) the
1652	  Grassmannian \(\mathrm{Gr}(r, d)\) is constructed by gluing
1653	  \(\binom{r}{d}\) affine charts \(U^I = \Spec\mathbb{Z}[X^I]\) indexed by
1654	  size-\(d\) subsets \(I \subseteq \{1, \dots, r\}\). The chart \(U^I\)
1655	  carries the matrix \(X^I\) of size \(d \times r\) whose \(I\)-th \(d \times d\)
1656	  minor is the identity, with the remaining \(d(r-d)\) entries free
1657	  variables (so \(U^I \cong \mathbb{A}^{d(r-d)}_{\mathbb{Z}}\)). The
1658	  transition maps \(\theta_{I,J} : U^I \cap U^J \to U^J \cap U^I\) defined
1659	  by \(X^J \mapsto (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\) on the locus where \(\det X^I_J\) is
1660	  invertible satisfy the cocycle condition
1661	  \(\theta_{I,K} = \theta_{I,J} \circ \theta_{J,K}\). The glued
1662	  \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec\mathbb{Z}\) is smooth of relative dimension
1663	  \(d(r-d)\). Separatedness is checked from the diagonal
1664	  \(\Delta_{I,J} \subset U^I \times U^J\) cut out by \(X^J_I X^I - X^J = 0\).
1665	  Properness follows from the valuative criterion for DVRs: given
1666	  \(\varphi : \Spec K \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) over a DVR \(R\) with fraction
1667	  field \(K\), factoring through some chart \(U^I\) via a ring map
1668	  \(f : \mathbb{Z}[X^I] \to K\), choose \(J\) minimising the valuation
1669	  \(\nu(f(P^I_J))\) of the minor; on the chart \(U^J\) all entries have
1670	  non-negative valuation, so \(f\) extends uniquely to \(\Spec R\).
1671	
1672	  The universal quotient \(u : \mathcal{O}_{\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)}^{\oplus r}
1673	  \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{U}\) is given on \(U^I\) by the matrix \(X^I\)
1674	  and glued by \(g_{I,J} = (X^I_J)^{-1} \in GL_d(U^I \cap U^J)\). The
1675	  determinant line bundle \(\det\mathcal{U}\) has transition functions
1676	  \(\det g_{I,J} = 1/P^I_J\); the global sections
1677	  \(\sigma_I \in \Gamma(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d), \det\mathcal{U})\) defined by
1678	  \(\sigma_I|_{U^J} = P^J_I\) form a base-point-free linear system separating
1679	  points over \(\Spec\mathbb{Z}\), giving an embedding into
1680	  \(\mathbb{P}^{\binom{r}{d}-1}_{\mathbb{Z}}\), which is closed by
1681	  properness. Representability of \(\mathrm{Grass}(r,d)\) is then verified
1682	  on the universal pair \((\mathrm{Gr}(r,d), u)\): a quotient
1683	  \(q : \mathcal{O}_T^{\oplus r} \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\) of
1684	  rank \(d\) on \(T\) is everywhere locally trivialised; on the locus where
1685	  the columns indexed by \(I\) are a basis the matrix of \(q\) has the form
1686	  \(X^I\), defining the classifying morphism \(T \to U^I \subset
1687	  \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\). Base change from \(\mathbb{Z}\) to a general noetherian
1688	  base \(S\) with a vector bundle \(V\) is direct: trivialise \(V\) on an open
1689	  cover and glue. For non-trivial \(V\) the same construction produces
1690	  \(\mathrm{Gr}_S(V, d)\) with Pl\"ucker embedding into
1691	  \(\mathbb{P}_S(\bigwedge^d V)\).
1692	\end{proof}
